abstract: This paper introduces Conversational Inoculation, a method for building resistance to misinformation through structured dialogue with an LLM-powered chatbot. The authors developed MindFort, a web-based system implementing three conditions—Reading (supportive defense), Writing (traditional refutation), and Chatbot (conversational inoculation via an agent named 'Forty')—and evaluated it in a within-subjects experiment with 65 participants across four topics (exercise/mental health, binge drinking, dental hygiene, and protecting nature). Results show that the Chatbot condition produced significantly greater resistance to misinformation than a no-treatment Control, and outperformed Reading and Writing when controlling for individual baseline susceptibility. Qualitative and linguistic analyses identified adaptability, independent thinking, trust-building, and interactional friction as key factors shaping the inoculation process.
key_claims: Conversational Inoculation, delivered via an LLM-powered chatbot, is a viable novel method for building resistance to misinformation.
When controlling for individual baseline susceptibility, conversational inoculation outperforms traditional Reading and Writing inoculation approaches.
The conversational format sustains user motivation and engagement at levels comparable to traditional methods without sacrificing effectiveness.
Effectiveness depends not only on argument content but on interaction design factors: scaffolding independent thinking, building trust through partnership, and minimizing interactional friction.
Inoculation effectiveness varies by topic, suggesting prompt and tone design should be tailored to subject matter.
methods: Developed MindFort, a Flask-based web application using GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-11-20, temperature 1) to implement a chatbot persona ('Forty') engineered via a structured system prompt grounded in inoculation theory and prompt-engineering literature.

Within-subjects online experiment with 65 participants (61 after exclusions) recruited via Prolific from English-speaking countries, compensated £12, with sample size justified by G*Power for Wilcoxon signed-rank test (d=.5, α=.05).

Each participant completed four lessons (one per topic: exercise/mental health, binge drinking, dental hygiene, protecting nature) in counterbalanced Latin Square order across four conditions: Control, Reading, Writing, Chatbot.

Each lesson followed a 5-stage inoculation protocol: pre-treatment certainty rating (15-point McGuire scale), treatment, mid-lesson certainty rating, strong counterattitudinal attack, post-attack certainty rating plus Intrinsic Motivation Inventory (IMI).
Quantitative analysis using Friedman tests, Bonferroni-corrected Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, and a linear mixed-effects model (lme4) with participants and topics as random effects.
Qualitative deductive content analysis of chatbot conversations and open feedback, plus LIWC-22 linguistic analysis benchmarked against the LMSYS-Chat-1M dataset (N=10,000 reference conversations).
findings: Chatbot condition produced significantly less post-attack certainty loss than Control (p=.001, r=-.33); pairwise differences with Reading (p=.07) and Writing (p=.08) were not significant.
When controlling for individual susceptibility (Inoculation Effectiveness measure), Chatbot significantly outperformed both Reading (p=.004) and Writing (p=.033).
The Chatbot treatment slightly increased mid-lesson certainty while the Writing treatment decreased it (difference significant, r=-.30, p=.006).
Inoculation effects varied by topic: exercise/mental health and binge drinking showed larger certainty drops than dental hygiene and protecting nature.
IMI scores did not differ significantly between conditions on most subscales; post-debriefing scores showed significant increases in Interest/Enjoyment, Perceived Competence, and Value/Usefulness.

LIWC analysis showed chatbot conversations were less Analytic, more Authentic, higher in Cognition, Conflict, Emotion, and Health words compared to reference chatbot dialogues, and significantly longer in word count.

For the binge drinking topic, LIWC 'Achieve' language strongly correlated with inoculation effectiveness (Spearman ρ=0.92, p=.008); no significant linguistic correlates emerged overall after Bonferroni correction.

Qualitative themes identified four mechanisms: adaptability of the chatbot to user attitudes, fostering of independent research and thinking, trust-building through partnership rather than authority, and interactional friction (technical glitches, disengagement) as a key barrier.
contributions: Introduces and operationalizes the concept of Conversational Inoculation as a paradigm distinct from traditional passive or written inoculation approaches.
Provides MindFort, an open-source web-based prototype and accompanying participant dataset for reproducibility and future research.
Offers empirical validation that LLM-powered conversational agents can deliver effective cognitive inoculation against health-related misinformation.
Identifies design factors (adaptability, trust, independence-fostering, friction reduction) that shape conversational inoculation outcomes, informing future HCI design.

Proposes future directions including multi-bot inoculation architectures separating threat and facilitation roles, personalized adaptive strategies, and inoculation targeting cognitive biases or self-efficacy rather than specific topics.
framing: The paper situates itself at the intersection of Cognitive Inoculation Theory (McGuire, Banas & Rains, Compton) and HCI research on conversational agents and misinformation interventions. It positions Conversational Inoculation as a response to limitations of traditional inoculation methods—particularly their inability to adapt to individual attitudes—and as an extension of recent gamified and LLM-assisted inoculation work (e.g., Bad News, Tang & Sergeeva, Malek et al.). The contribution is framed as advancing scalable, human-centered defenses against misinformation in an era of generative AI.
